title: feat: Add non-secret metadata for registered components via Metadata API
body:
<!-- If you need to report a security issue please visit https://docs.dapr.io/operations/support/support-security-issues -->

## In what area(s)?

<!-- Remove the '> ' to select -->

/area runtime

## Describe the feature
The SDKs and applications consuming these SDKs might realize value in being able to introspectively access information about the components it's only using internally by name. While secret values obviously shouldn't be leaked through this, it'd be useful to be able to access not just reference values, but the actual resolved values in key/value format. Ideally, if this is a secret, it should return something like "<secret>" to indicate there _is_ a value, it's just not something that'll be communicated over the API.

The [Metadata API response component](https://docs.dapr.io/reference/api/metadata_api/#http-response-body) already contains the name, type, version and capabilities (for Bindngs and State Management components), but should be extended to include a `metadata` property reflecting the resolved metadata object from the component.

## Release Note
<!-- How should this new feature be announced in our release notes? It can be populated later. -->
<!-- Keep it as a single line. Examples: -->

<!-- RELEASE NOTE: **ADD** New feature in Dapr. -->
<!-- RELEASE NOTE: **FIX** Bug in runtime. -->
<!-- RELEASE NOTE: **UPDATE** Runtime dependency. -->

RELEASE NOTE: **ADD** Adding component metadata to Metadata API

comments.body:
Hey @WhitWaldo thanks for the issue!

Dumping resolved component metadata makes every component's internal schema a public contract on the metadata API. Renames, deprecations, and new fields in any component would then ripple into a generic introspection surface, and it would be very difficult to know what to consider secret or not pending a user's profile of Dapr.

If the underlying need is agent/client-side discovery, I think the better cut is exposing capabilities, not config. Each component would declare a small, versioned descriptor (for conversation: supports tool calling, structured output, streaming, default model hint, etc.) and the metadata API would surface those. Anything spec-level that a client genuinely needs can be read from the component YAML directly.

It's worth noting that capabilities are already surfaced in the metadata API as a per-component field. State stores expose FeatureETag, FeatureTransactional, FeatureQueryAPI, FeatureTTL, and pub/sub, bindings, and secret stores follow the same pattern via their Features() method.

Conversation components are the gap. If this is stemming from an agent POV, the natural path is revisiting Features() on the Conversation interface with a typed enum along the lines of FeatureToolCalling, FeatureStreaming, FeatureStructuredOutput, FeatureVision, FeaturePromptCaching. The caveat is that this maps less cleanly than it does for state stores and the other resource types. Most modern LLM providers support most of these, and the meaningful differentiation often lives at the model level (e.g. vision support varies by model within the same provider) rather than at the component level, something that we wouldn't know. So we'd need to decide whether the descriptor describes the component's configured model or the provider's full envelope.

If it's presented in the manner I proposed, just as a bag of key/value pairs reflecting the metadata, there's no public contract to maintain. It's just a read-only view of what the components have resolved to in the runtime and is not subject to shifting names but just reflecting whatever is currently there. With regards to secrets, we already know what those are - they're anything characterized with a secret ref. Those resolved values obviously should not be leaked, but anything else should be fair game.

But your response proposes a dramatically broader change than what I'm asking for. That would be a dramatically heavier lift and one I don't initially support as written for reasons characterized below.

You suggest using the capabilities reflected in the State or Bindings spec to reflect those capabilities of any given model and I would caution against this because this is primarily used as a gating mechanism in Dapr itself for other building blocks that take a dependency on it (e.g. an Actor requires a state store that supports transactions). It doesn't reflect what the underlying component actually can do, only what the implementation of it as represented in components-contrib supports or doesn't.

But individual Conversational models do not have representation in components-contrib. They are supported on a per-provider basis instead which means that such flags for functional support would have to be applied to the provider, not the model. This might work for OpenAI or Anthropic and their relatively small model families, but not for the abstractions like Ollama. Further, your proposed capability flags would not be known or set by Dapr itself and would have to be set by developers themselves (attesting to the functionality of the model they name) which risks inaccuracy (especially if the infrastructure team ever changes the model used). To my knowledge, no other component necessarily requires a manual rebuild of all the metadata just because the value of one key changes. It feels like this would be prone to unnecessary footguns.

Rather, sticking to the Conversation concept, the SDK and in turn, the application consuming it know nothing about the details of the components they implement - just their name, and by design. The component loaded in Dapr is the best source of truth of what the component can do because it's how Dapr will be using it. But the developer consuming the model might like to know which model a given component implements so it can itself make better inferences about how it should be used. These developers can maintain their own internal registries with model information (parameter count, what functionality it supports, proposed roles for it) based on the name of the model alone and I strongly advise Dapr not get into the world of trying to maintain such a registry ourselves.
